en.extract:
The snail kite (Rostrhamus sociabilis) is a bird of prey within the family Accipitridae, which also includes the eagles, hawks, and Old World vultures. Its relative, the slender-billed kite, is now again placed in Helicolestes, making the genus Rostrhamus monotypic. Usually, it is placed in the Milvine kites, but the validity of that grouping is under investigation.


Taxonomy
Lerner and Mindell (2005) found R. sociabilis sister to Geranospiza caerulescens, and that those two along with Ictinea plumbea were basal to both the Buteogallus and Buteo clades. They concluded that Rostrhamus belonged in Buteoninae (sensu stricto) and not in Milvinae, but noted that more investigation was needed. 


Distribution
The snail kite breeds in tropical South America, the Caribbean, and central and southern Florida in the United States. It is resident all-year round in most of its range, but the southernmost population migrates north in winter and the Caribbean birds disperse widely outside the breeding season.


Description

Snail kites are 36 to 48 cm (14 to 19 in) long with a 99–120 cm (39–47 in) wingspan. They weigh from 300 to 570 g (11 to 20 oz). There is very limited sexual dimorphism, with the female averaging only 3% larger than the male. They have long, broad, and rounded wings, which measure 29–33 cm (11–13 in) each. Its tail is long, at 16–21 cm (6.3–8.3 in), with a white rump and undertail coverts. The dark, deeply hooked beak, measuring 2.9–4 cm (1.1–1.6 in) is an adaptation to its diet. The tarsus is relatively long as well, measuring 3.6–5.7 cm (1.4–2.2 in).
The adult male has dark blue-gray plumage with darker flight feathers. The legs and cere are red. The adult female has dark brown upperparts and heavily streaked pale underparts. She has a whitish face with darker areas behind and above the eye. The legs and cere are yellow or orange. The juvenile is similar to an adult female, but the crown is streaked. Adults have red or orangish-brown irises, while juveniles have dark brown irises.
It flies slowly with its head facing downwards, looking for its main food, the large apple snails. For this reason, it is considered a molluscivore.


Breeding
It nests in a bush or on the ground, laying three to four eggs.


Conservation

The snail kite is a locally endangered species in the Florida Everglades, with a population of less than 400 breeding pairs. Research has demonstrated that water-level control in the Everglades is depleting the population of apple snails. However, this species is not generally threatened over its extensive range.
In fact, it might be locally increasing in numbers, such as in Central America. In El Salvador, it was first recorded in 1996. Since then, it has been regularly sighted, including immature birds, suggesting a resident breeding population might already exist in that country. On the other hand, most records are outside the breeding season, more indicative of post-breeding dispersal. In El Salvador, the species can be observed during the winter months at Embalse Cerrón Grande, Laguna El Jocotal, and especially Lago de Güija. Pomacea flagellata apple snails were propagated in El Salvador between 1982 and 1986 as food for fish stocks, and it seems that the widespread presence of high numbers of these snails has not gone unnoticed by the snail kite.


In the Everglades
Due to the drainage and habitat destruction of the Everglades, they were one of the first species put on the US Fish and Wildlife Service's endangered species list on 11 March 1967. The snail kite continued to decline, reaching a population of less than 800 in 2007. One factor for the decline in the 2000s was the introduction of the invasive South American snail Pomacea maculata, which were five times bigger than the native Pomacea paludosa species, and most kites could not eat the new snails. However, the kites quickly evolved to be 12% bigger to adapt to the new food source. This population gradually rebounded, reaching a count of 3,000 snail kites in 2022. Everglades conservation efforts over the course of 30 years and costing over US$20 billion also contributed to restoring native vegetation of the snail kites' habitats  and flow of water in marshes.


Diet

This is a gregarious bird of freshwater wetlands, forming large winter roosts. Its diet consists almost exclusively of apple snails, especially the species Pomacea diffusa, P. maculata and P. paludosa in Florida, other species of Pomacea (P. doliodes) elsewhere in its range, and species of the genus Marisa (M. cornuarietis).
Snail kites have been observed eating other prey items in Florida, including other freshwater snail species (such as the banded mystery snail), crayfish in the genus Procambarus, crabs in the genera Dilocarcinus and Poppiana (P. dentata), black crappie, ring-necked snakes, small turtles (including the common musk turtle, striped mud turtle, coastal plain cooter, Florida red-bellied cooter, Florida softshell turtle, and other unidentified species), rodents and carcasses (based only on a single reported case of a dead American coot). It is believed that snail kites turn to these alternatives only when apple snails become scarce, such as during drought, but further study is needed. On 14 May 2007, a birder photographed a snail kite feeding at a red swamp crayfish farm in Clarendon County, South Carolina.
The presence of the large introduced Pomacea maculata in Florida has led the snail kites in North America to develop larger bodies and beaks to better eat the snail, a case of rapid evolution. These non-native snails provide a better food source than the smaller native snails and have had a positive effect on the kites' populations.


Predation
In Florida, snail kites may be eaten by some growth stage of invasive snakes such as Burmese pythons, reticulated pythons, Southern African rock pythons, Central African rock pythons, boa constrictors, yellow anacondas, Bolivian anacondas, dark-spotted anacondas, and green anacondas.


References


External links
 Media related to Rostrhamus sociabilis at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Rostrhamus sociabilis at Wikispecies
Snail Kite Information at Great Florida Birding & Wildlife Trail
BirdLife species factsheet for Rostrhamus sociabilis
"Rostrhamus sociabilis". Avibase. 
"Snail kite media". Internet Bird Collection.
Snail kite photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Interactive range map of Rostrhamus sociabilis at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of Snail kite on Xeno-canto.

The '''snail kite''' ('''''Rostrhamus sociabilis''''') is a [[bird of prey]] within the family [[Accipitridae]], which also includes the [[eagle]]s, [[hawk]]s, and [[Old World vulture]]s. Its relative, the [[slender-billed kite]], is now again placed in ''Helicolestes'', making the [[genus]] '''''Rostrhamus''''' [[monotypic]]. Usually, it is placed in the [[milvinae|Milvine kites]], but the validity of that grouping is under investigation.<!-- presumably invalid - upcoming paper in Journal of Avian Biology -->

==Taxonomy==
Lerner and Mindell (2005) found ''R. sociabilis'' sister to ''[[Crane hawk|Geranospiza caerulescens]]'', and that those two along with ''[[Plumbeous kite|Ictinea plumbea]]'' were basal to both the ''[[Buteogallus]]'' and ''[[Buteo]]'' clades. They concluded that ''Rostrhamus'' belonged in [[Buteoninae]] (sensu stricto) and not in [[Milvinae]], but noted that more investigation was needed.<ref name="Lerner2005" /> <!-- to do: find papers that focus on buteos, and see if they included Rostrhamus, etc. -->

==Distribution==
The snail kite breeds in tropical [[South America]], the [[Caribbean]], and central and southern [[Florida]] in the [[United States]]. It is resident all-year round in most of its range, but the southernmost population migrates north in winter and the Caribbean birds disperse widely outside the breeding season.{{Citation needed|date=January 2026}}

==Description==
[[File:Rostrhamus sociabilis 110977267 cropped.jpg|thumb|left|Juvenile]][[File:Female Snail Kite 0168 2.jpg|left|thumb|Female Snail Kite, Lake Okeechobee, Florida]][[File:Male Snail Kite 0235.jpg|left|thumb|Male Snail Kite, Lake Okeechobee, Florida]]
Snail kites are {{convert|36|to|48|cm|in|abbr=on}} long with a {{convert|99|-|120|cm|in|abbr=on}} wingspan. They weigh from {{convert|300|to|570|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name=AAB/><ref name=Ferguson-Lees2001/> There is very limited sexual dimorphism, with the female averaging only 3% larger than the male. They have long, broad, and rounded wings, which measure {{convert|29|-|33|cm|in|abbr=on}} each. Its tail is long, at {{convert|16|-|21|cm|in|abbr=on}}, with a white rump and undertail coverts. The dark, deeply hooked beak, measuring {{convert|2.9|-|4|cm|in|abbr=on}} is an adaptation to its diet. The [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] is relatively long as well, measuring {{convert|3.6|-|5.7|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees2001/>

The adult male has dark blue-gray [[plumage]] with darker [[flight feather]]s. The legs and [[cere]] are red. The adult female has dark brown upperparts and heavily streaked pale underparts. She has a whitish face with darker areas behind and above the eye. The legs and cere are yellow or orange. The juvenile is similar to an adult female, but the crown is streaked. Adults have red or orangish-brown irises, while juveniles have dark brown irises.<ref>{{cite web |title=All About Snail Kites |url=https://www.florida-ecology.com/all-about-snail-kites |website=Sharp-Eatman Nature Photography |access-date=26 January 2021 |language=en}}</ref>

It flies slowly with its head facing downwards, looking for its main food, the large [[Ampullariidae|apple snails]]. For this reason, it is considered a [[molluscivore]].

==Breeding==
It nests in a [[Shrub|bush]] or on the ground, laying three to four [[bird egg|eggs]].

==Conservation==
[[File:Adult male, Florida.jpg|thumb|left|Adult male Everglades snail kite in Joe Overstreet Landing, Florida.]]

The snail kite is a locally endangered species in the Florida [[Everglades]], with a population of less than 400 breeding pairs. Research has demonstrated that [[Draining and development of the Everglades|water-level control]] in the Everglades is depleting the population of apple snails.<ref name=Okeechobee/> However, this [[species]] is not generally threatened over its extensive range.

In fact, it might be locally increasing in numbers, such as in [[Central America]]. In [[El Salvador]], it was first recorded in 1996. Since then, it has been regularly sighted, including immature birds, suggesting a resident breeding population might already exist in that country. On the other hand, most records are outside the breeding season, more indicative of post-breeding dispersal. In El Salvador, the species can be observed during the winter months at [[Cerrón Grande Hydroelectric Dam#Cerrón Grande Lake|Embalse Cerrón Grande]], Laguna El Jocotal, and especially [[Lago de Güija]]. ''[[Pomacea flagellata]]'' apple snails were propagated in El Salvador between 1982 and 1986 as food for fish stocks, and it seems that the widespread presence of high numbers of these snails has not gone unnoticed by the snail kite.<ref name="Herrera2006" />

===In the Everglades===
Due to the [[Everglades#Drainage|drainage]] and habitat destruction of the Everglades, they were one of the first species put on the [[US Fish and Wildlife Service]]'s [[Endangered Species Act of 1973#Endangered Species Preservation Act of 1966|endangered species list]]<ref name="invasive snail">{{cite web |url=https://www.npr.org/2023/03/14/1157862843/endangered-snail-kite-invasive-apple-snail-florida |title=In Florida, an invasive snail is helping save an endangered bird |last=Allen |first=Greg |date=2023-03-14 |website=[[NPR]] |publisher=NPR |language=en |access-date=2023-03-19}}</ref> on 11 March 1967.<ref name="ecos">{{cite web |url=https://ecos.fws.gov/ecp/species/7713 |title=Everglade snail kite (''Rostrhamus sociabilis plumbeus'') |author=<!--Author not stated--> |date= |website=Environmental Conservation Online System |publisher=US Fish & Wildlife Service |language=en |access-date=2023-03-19}}</ref> The snail kite continued to decline, reaching a population of less than 800 in 2007. One factor for the decline in the 2000s was the introduction of the invasive [[South America|South American]] snail ''[[Pomacea maculata]]'', which were five times bigger than the native ''[[Pomacea paludosa]]'' species, and most kites could not eat the new snails.<ref name='WNET'>{{Cite news |url=https://www.pbs.org/wnet/nature/this-bird-is-evolving-right-in-front-of-us/30077/ |title=This Bird Is Evolving Right in Front of Us |access-date=2025-05-12 |newspaper=Nature The WNET Group }}</ref> However, the kites quickly [[Evolution|evolved]] to be 12% bigger to [[Adaptation|adapt]] to the new food source.<ref name='PBS'>{{Cite news |url=https://az.pbslearningmedia.org/resource/snail-kites-evolve-to-make-comeback-video/nature-season-42-raptors-a-fistful-of-daggers/ |title=Snail Kites Evolve to Make Surprising Comeback |access-date=2025-05-12 |newspaper=Nature PBS }}</ref><ref name="Rogers">{{cite web |url=https://wildlife.org/invasive-snails-rescue-endangered-bird-of-prey/ |title=Invasive snails rescue endangered bird of prey |date=October 11, 2016 |author=Nala Rogers |publisher=The Wildlife Society }}</ref> This population gradually rebounded, reaching a count of 3,000 snail kites in 2022. Everglades conservation efforts over the course of 30 years and costing over US$20 billion also contributed to restoring native vegetation of the snail kites' habitats  and flow of water in marshes.<ref name="invasive snail"/>

==Diet==
[[File:Snail Kite, Chagres lake, Panama imported from iNaturalist photo 31341912.jpg|thumb|Eating a snail]]
This is a gregarious bird of freshwater [[wetland]]s, forming large winter roosts. Its diet consists almost exclusively of [[Ampullariidae|apple snails]], especially the species ''[[Pomacea diffusa]]'', ''[[Pomacea maculata|P. maculata]]'' and ''[[Pomacea paludosa|P. paludosa]]'' in Florida, other species of ''[[Pomacea]]'' (''[[Pomacea doliodes|P. doliodes]]'') elsewhere in its range, and species of the genus ''[[Marisa (gastropod)|Marisa]]'' (''[[Marisa cornuarietis|M. cornuarietis]]'').<ref name="animaldiversity.org">{{Cite web|url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Rostrhamus_sociabilis/|title = Rostrhamus sociabilis (Everglade kite)| website=[[Animal Diversity Web]] }}</ref><ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001">Ferguson-Lees, J. & Christie, D. A. & Franklin, K. & Mead, D. & Burton, P. (2001). ''Raptors of the World''. Helm Identification Guides.</ref><ref>Reichert, B. E., Cattau, C. E., Fletcher, J. R. J., W, S. J. P., A, R. J. J., & Bennetts, R. E. (2020). Snail Kite (Rostrhamus sociabilis), version 1.0. birdsoftheworld.org. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.snakit.01</ref>

Snail kites have been observed eating other prey items in Florida, including other [[freshwater snail]] species (such as the [[Viviparus georgianus|banded mystery snail]]), [[crayfish]] in the genus ''[[Procambarus]]'', [[crab]]s in the genera ''Dilocarcinus'' and ''Poppiana'' (''P. dentata''), [[black crappie]], [[ring-necked snake]]s, small [[turtle]]s (including the [[Sternotherus odoratus|common musk turtle]], [[striped mud turtle]], [[coastal plain cooter]], [[Florida red-bellied cooter]], [[Florida softshell turtle]], and other unidentified species), [[rodent]]s and [[Carrion|carcass]]es (based only on a single reported case of a dead [[American coot]]).<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/kites/snail-kite | title=Snail Kite | the Peregrine Fund }}</ref><ref name="Ferguson-Lees, J. 2001"/><ref name="animaldiversity.org"/><ref>Reichert, B. E., Cattau, C. E., Fletcher, J. R. J., W, S. J. P., A, R. J. J., & Bennetts, R. E. (2020). Snail Kite (Rostrhamus sociabilis), version 1.0. birdsoftheworld.org. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.snakit.01</ref> It is believed that snail kites turn to these alternatives only when apple snails become scarce, such as during drought,<ref name=Davis1994/> but further study is needed. On 14 May 2007, a [[Birdwatching|birder]] photographed a snail kite feeding at a [[Procambarus clarkii|red swamp crayfish]] farm in [[Clarendon County, South Carolina]].<ref name=Pogatchnik2007/><ref name=Rimini/>

The presence of the large [[Introduced species|introduced]] ''Pomacea maculata'' in Florida has led the snail kites in North America to develop larger bodies and beaks to better eat the snail, a case of rapid [[evolution]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Cattau |display-authors=etal |date=27 Nov 2017 |title=Rapid morphological change of a top predator with the invasion of a novel prey |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41559-017-0378-1 |journal=Nature Ecology & Evolution |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=108–115 |doi=10.1038/s41559-017-0378-1 |pmid=29180705 |s2cid=20394037 |via=Nature|url-access=subscription }}</ref> These non-native snails provide a better food source than the smaller native snails and have had a positive effect on the kites' populations.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Poli |display-authors=etal |date=22 Jun 2022 |title=An invasive prey provides long-lasting silver spoon effects for an endangered predator |journal=  Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|volume=289 |issue=1977 |article-number=20220820 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2022.0820 |pmid=35730154 |pmc=9233927 |s2cid=249891466 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2022RSPSB.28920820P }}</ref>

==Predation==
In Florida, snail kites may be eaten by some growth stage of invasive snakes such as [[Burmese python]]s, [[reticulated python]]s, [[Southern African rock python]]s, [[Central African rock python]]s, [[boa constrictor]]s, [[yellow anaconda]]s, [[Bolivian anaconda]]s, [[dark-spotted anaconda]]s, and [[green anaconda]]s.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Final Environmental Assessment For The Large Constrictor Snakes Listed As Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act|date= January 2012|publisher=U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service}}</ref>
